class_schedule.topic: Character Archetypes and Visual Design Foundations
learning_objectives: Identify and apply classical character archetypes to game character design
Understand how silhouette, shape language, and color communicate character traits at a glance
Analyze iconic game characters to extract their visual design principles
readings: The Art of Game Design: A Book of Lenses Ch. 19-20 (Jesse Schell)
Character Design from the Ground Up (Kevin Crossley) Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: A player forms an opinion about a character in under two seconds — before any dialogue, before any backstory. That first impression comes from visual design: silhouette, shape language, color, and proportion. Today we study the principles that make characters readable, memorable, and emotionally resonant.
key_concepts: The 12 Jungian archetypes mapped to game characters: Hero, Mentor, Shadow, Trickster, etc.
Shape language: circles convey friendliness (Kirby), triangles convey danger (Ganondorf), squares convey stability (Minecraft Steve)
Silhouette test: a well-designed character is identifiable from a solid black outline alone
Color psychology in character design: warm/cool palettes, saturation as power indicator, hue as faction signifier
Proportion exaggeration: how stylized proportions (big head, small body) amplify personality traits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Silhouette analysis of 20 iconic game characters: identifying which are readable at thumbnail scale and extracting the shape language that makes them work
setup_requirements: Digital drawing tablet or pen and paper for sketching exercises
Adobe Photoshop, Clip Studio Paint, or Procreate
Reference image folder of assigned characters (provided in course materials)

review.value: The psychological associations of basic geometric shapes. Circles and curves suggest approachability and softness. Triangles and sharp angles suggest aggression and danger. Squares and rectangles suggest strength and reliability.

Fill the character's outline with solid black. If a viewer can identify the character, their weapon, and their general personality from the silhouette alone, the design has strong readability. Characters that fail this test are visually confused in gameplay.

learning_objectives: Produce a professional character sheet with turnaround views, expression studies, and callouts
Understand how character sheets serve as the binding contract between concept art and 3D modeling
Document proportions, color palettes, and material specifications for production handoff
readings: Character Design from the Ground Up Ch. 5-6 (Kevin Crossley)

lecture_notes.introduction: A concept sketch is a starting point. A character sheet is a blueprint. In a production pipeline, the character sheet is what a 3D modeler, rigger, and animator use to build the final asset. Ambiguity in the sheet means inconsistency in the game.
key_concepts: Turnaround views: front, 3/4, side, and back at consistent scale on a proportion grid
Expression sheet: 6-8 facial expressions showing the range of the character's emotional communication
Costume and prop callouts: zoomed-in details of accessories, weapons, and material properties
Color palette specification: exact hex/RGB values for skin, clothing, hair, and accent colors
Scale reference: the character shown next to other characters and environment objects for size context
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a complete character sheet for an original character in a live drawing session, starting from a rough concept and building up to production-ready turnarounds
setup_requirements: Digital drawing application with layer support
Proportion grid template (provided in course materials)
Color picker and swatch palette tool

review.value: A set of drawings showing the character from front, side (profile), 3/4, and back angles, all at the same scale on a horizontal baseline grid. Turnarounds eliminate guesswork for the 3D modeler by defining every angle explicitly.

A grid of 6-8 head drawings showing key emotions (neutral, happy, angry, sad, surprised, determined, etc.). Expression sheets define the character's emotional range and ensure facial animation targets match the concept artist's intent.

learning_objectives: Construct character personality profiles that drive consistent in-game behavior
Design character motivations that create meaningful player investment
Write character dialogue that reveals personality through voice rather than exposition
readings: The Art of Game Design Ch. 21 (Jesse Schell)
Writing for Video Games (Steve Ince) Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Visual design gets the player's attention. Personality keeps it. A character's motivation — what they want, what they fear, what they are willing to sacrifice — is the engine that drives player investment. Today we build the internal life that makes characters feel like people rather than quest dispensers.
key_concepts: The motivation triad: want (conscious goal), need (unconscious flaw to overcome), fear (the consequence of failure)
Character voice: vocabulary, sentence structure, speech patterns, and verbal tics that make dialogue attributable without a name tag
Show-don't-tell in interactive media: reveal personality through actions, reactions, and choices rather than exposition dumps
Character arcs in games: how player agency interacts with authored character growth
The personality diamond: four axes (warmth/coldness, confidence/doubt, idealism/pragmatism, introversion/extroversion) that define a character's behavioral space
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building personality profiles for three characters (a mentor, an antagonist, and a companion) for a hypothetical RPG, then writing three dialogue exchanges where personality differences drive the conflict
setup_requirements: Character profile template (provided in course materials)
Word processor or screenwriting software (WriterSolo, Final Draft, or Google Docs)

review.value: Want: what the character consciously pursues (find the artifact). Need: what they must actually learn or overcome (trust others). Fear: what happens if they fail (becoming like the villain). The tension between want and need creates character depth.

A character's unique speech pattern. A grizzled soldier uses short, declarative sentences with military jargon. A nervous scholar uses long, hedging sentences with academic vocabulary. Good character voice means the reader can identify the speaker without attribution.

learning_objectives: Apply the 12 principles of animation to game character movement
Design facial animation blend shape sets for real-time expression rendering
Understand how animation style communicates personality as strongly as visual design
readings: The Animator's Survival Kit Ch. 1-3 (Richard Williams)
Game Anim: Video Game Animation Explained Ch. 4-6 (Jonathan Cooper)

lecture_notes.introduction: Animation is where a character truly comes alive. A character can look perfect in a T-pose and feel lifeless in motion. The way a character walks, idles, reacts, and emotes defines their personality as much as their dialogue. In games, animation must also serve gameplay: communicating state, telegraphing attacks, and providing feedback.
key_concepts: The 12 principles of animation applied to games: squash and stretch, anticipation, follow-through, and their role in attack telegraphing
Idle animations as personality statements: how a character stands when the player is not moving reveals who they are
Facial Action Coding System (FACS): the anatomical basis for blend shapes mapped to facial muscles
Blend shape libraries: 30-52 shapes covering the FACS Action Units for real-time facial animation
Animation state machines: connecting idle, locomotion, combat, and emote animation layers with blend trees
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rigging a character face in Blender with 8 core FACS blend shapes (brow raise, brow lower, eye squint, eye wide, smile, frown, jaw open, lip pucker), then driving them with a simple emotion controller
setup_requirements: Blender 3.6+ with a rigged character head model (provided in course materials)
Understanding of mesh deformation and shape keys
Reference photos of facial expressions (Ekman FACS reference set)

review.value: The Facial Action Coding System defines 46 Action Units (AUs) corresponding to individual facial muscle movements. In games, 30-52 blend shapes modeled from these AUs are combined additively to produce any facial expression in real time.

A preparatory movement in the opposite direction before the main action (pulling back before a punch, crouching before a jump). In games, anticipation frames serve as attack telegraphs, giving players readable visual cues to react to.

learning_objectives: Design NPCs that feel like inhabitants of the world rather than quest machines
Understand companion AI design patterns for non-player allies
Balance companion utility with the player's sense of agency and accomplishment
readings: GDC 2014: 'Building Companion AI in The Last of Us' (Max Dyckhoff)

lecture_notes.introduction: NPCs are the population of your game world. Bad NPCs break immersion faster than any graphical flaw. A shopkeeper who stares at a wall, a companion who blocks doorways, a quest-giver who repeats the same line — these are design failures, not AI failures. Today we study NPCs as designed characters, not just systems.
key_concepts: NPC behavior layers: ambient (idle activities), reactive (respond to player/world events), interactive (conversation, trading, questing)
Daily schedule systems: NPCs with routines (sleep, work, eat, socialize) that make the world feel alive
Companion AI design pillars: never block the player, never steal kills, always be useful, always feel present
The Elizabeth principle (BioShock Infinite): companions should add to gameplay through environmental interaction, resource sharing, and emotional presence without ever being a liability
Barks and systemic dialogue: short contextual voice lines triggered by game state (entering a new area, spotting danger, finding loot) that make companions feel aware
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a companion character from personality through behavior: defining their personality diamond, writing 20 contextual barks, and mapping their behavior state machine (following, combat support, exploration commentary, idle activities)
setup_requirements: Behavior tree editor (Unreal Engine or custom tool)

review.value: Named after Elizabeth from BioShock Infinite: a companion character should enhance the player's experience through emotional presence and occasional gameplay aid without ever becoming a burden. The player should never have to babysit, protect, or work around their companion.

Short voice lines (2-8 seconds) triggered by game state rather than player interaction. A companion saying 'looks like rain' when weather changes or 'careful, I hear something' near enemies creates the illusion of awareness. A library of 50-100 barks with cooldown timers prevents repetition.

class_schedule.topic: Character Design Integration and Portfolio Preparation
learning_objectives: Integrate visual design, personality, animation, and AI behavior into a cohesive character package
Evaluate character designs against production readiness criteria
Present character work in a professional portfolio format suitable for industry review
readings: The Art of Game Design Ch. 22 (Jesse Schell)

lecture_notes.introduction: A character is not a concept sketch, a personality profile, a rig, or a behavior tree — it is all of these working in concert. The final session of this unit focuses on integration: ensuring that visual design, personality, animation style, and AI behavior tell the same story about who this character is.
key_concepts: Design consistency audit: checking that shape language, color palette, animation style, and dialogue voice all reinforce the same character traits
The character pipeline review: concept art to model to rig to animation to behavior tree — ensuring each stage preserves the original design intent
Portfolio presentation standards: character sheets, turnarounds, animation reels, and behavior documentation formatted for industry review

Character design pitfalls: overdesign (too many details lose readability), underdesign (generic characters blend into the background), inconsistency (visual design says warrior, dialogue says scholar)
Iteration and feedback: presenting character designs to peers and incorporating critique without losing the core identity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full integration review of a course-long character project: walking through concept art, character sheet, personality profile, animation tests, and companion behavior design, identifying where the elements align and where they conflict
setup_requirements: All character deliverables from previous sessions compiled into a presentation

review.value: A systematic check that every aspect of a character reinforces the same identity. If the visual design uses round, soft shapes (friendly), the walk cycle should not be stiff and militaristic (threatening). Inconsistencies between visual, audio, and behavioral design create uncanny dissonance.

Industry-standard character portfolios include: high-resolution character sheet with turnarounds, expression studies, color callouts, and scale reference; a 30-60 second animation reel showing key poses and locomotion; and a one-page design brief summarizing the character's role, personality, and design rationale.

quizzes.topic: Character Archetypes, Visual Design, and Character Sheets

questions.question: You are designing a villain character for a fantasy RPG. The character is a cunning, manipulative sorcerer who avoids direct confrontation. Describe the shape language, color palette, and silhouette characteristics you would use, and explain how each choice communicates the character's personality to the player before any dialogue occurs.
questions.answer: Shape language: predominantly triangular — sharp shoulders, pointed collar, angular facial features, tapered fingers. Triangles communicate danger and aggression, and when combined with elongated proportions, they suggest intelligence and precision rather than brute force. Color palette: deep purples and blacks as primary colors (mystery, power, corruption) with acid green or gold accents (toxicity or greed). Low saturation on the body with high saturation on focal points (glowing eyes, enchanted jewelry) draws attention to the sources of magical power. Silhouette: tall and narrow with a distinctive asymmetric feature (one oversized sleeve, a staff, or a flowing cape that breaks the body outline). The silhouette should be instantly distinguishable from the hero's broader, more grounded shape at any distance.

rubric.key: Appropriate shape language with reasoning tied to personality
Color palette choices with psychological justification
Silhouette design that is distinctive and readable

questions.question: Explain why a character sheet requires turnaround views drawn on a proportion grid at consistent scale, rather than just a single front-facing illustration. Who uses the character sheet in production and what specific problems does it prevent?
questions.answer: A single front-facing illustration leaves the 3D modeler guessing about the character's profile, back design, and depth proportions. The proportion grid ensures all views are drawn at identical scale so the modeler can match measurements exactly. Turnarounds are used by: (1) the 3D modeler to build the base mesh with correct proportions from every angle, (2) the texture artist to paint consistent details across UV maps, (3) the rigger to understand joint placement and deformation volumes, and (4) the animator to understand weight distribution and silhouette during movement. Without turnarounds, common problems include: asymmetric models, incorrect proportions when viewed from the side, details on the back that contradict the front, and rigs that deform incorrectly because joint placement was guessed.

rubric.key: Explains the limitation of a single view (guesswork for 3D pipeline)
Identifies at least 2 production roles that use the character sheet
Describes specific problems prevented by consistent-scale turnarounds

quizzes.topic: Character Animation, NPC Design, and Companion AI

questions.question: Explain how the animation principle of anticipation serves a dual purpose in game design: both as an aesthetic animation principle and as a gameplay communication tool. Give a specific example of an enemy attack where removing the anticipation frames would make the game feel unfair.
questions.answer: Aesthetically, anticipation makes movement feel physically grounded and satisfying — a character crouches before jumping, pulls back before swinging. Without it, actions feel weightless and robotic. As a gameplay tool, anticipation frames are attack telegraphs that give the player time to read and react. For example, in Dark Souls, a large enemy's overhead slam has 15-20 frames of wind-up (raising the weapon high, shifting weight to the back foot) before the fast downswing. These anticipation frames are the player's entire reaction window for dodging. If the attack skipped straight to the downswing with no wind-up, the player would have zero frames to react, making the attack unavoidable and the game feel unfair regardless of the player's skill level. The duration of anticipation frames directly controls the difficulty: longer telegraphs make attacks easier to dodge.

rubric.key: Explains anticipation as aesthetic principle (weight, physicality)
Explains anticipation as gameplay telegraph (reaction window)
Provides a specific example showing why removal makes the game unfair

questions.question: Describe three specific design rules for companion AI that prevent the companion from becoming a liability to the player. For each rule, give an example of a game that violates it and explain the negative player experience that results.
questions.answer: Rule 1: Never block the player's movement path. The companion must have avoidance behavior that yields doorways and corridors to the player. Violation: In early Resident Evil 5, Sheva would stand in doorways during combat, trapping the player in kill zones. Players felt frustrated because death was caused by their ally, not the enemies. Rule 2: Never steal the player's kills or glory moments. The companion should assist but allow the player to land finishing blows on significant enemies. Violation: In some Skyrim companion configurations, followers would rush ahead and kill enemies before the player engaged, making the player feel like a spectator in their own game. Rule 3: Never require the player to micromanage the companion's survival. The companion should be functionally invulnerable or capable of self-preservation. Violation: In many escort missions (early Halo, Dead Rising), the escorted NPC would walk into enemy fire and die, forcing mission failure for something outside the player's control. This creates helpless frustration.

rubric.key: Three distinct, specific design rules stated clearly
Valid game examples that actually violate each rule
Clear explanation of the negative player experience for each violation

major_assignments.title: Complete Character Design Package
major_assignments.description: Create a complete, production-ready character design package for an original game character. The package must include concept exploration sketches, a finalized character sheet with turnaround views, an expression sheet, a personality profile with motivation triad and dialogue samples, and a color/material specification document. The character must be designed for a specific game genre and role (e.g., RPG companion, fighting game roster member, platformer protagonist), and every design choice must be justified in terms of that role.
objectives: Demonstrate mastery of visual character design principles including shape language, color theory, and silhouette readability
Create a personality profile with depth, internal conflict, and a distinct character voice
Produce production-quality character sheets that a 3D modeler could use without additional guidance
Justify every design decision in terms of the character's role, genre, and target audience
deliverables: Concept exploration: 10-15 thumbnail sketches showing design variations
Character sheet: front, 3/4, side, and back turnaround views on a proportion grid
Expression sheet: 8 facial expressions covering the character's emotional range
Personality profile: motivation triad, personality diamond, 5 sample dialogue lines in character voice
Design rationale document (1-2 pages) explaining how visual and narrative choices serve the character's game role

criteria: Shape language is consistent and communicates the character's personality
Color palette is intentional and supports the character's role and faction
Silhouette is distinctive and readable at small scale
Proportions are consistent across all turnaround views
Motivation triad is complete with meaningful internal conflict between want and need
Character voice is distinct and consistent across all dialogue samples
Personality diamond axes are reflected in both visual design and written characterization
Character has enough depth to sustain player interest beyond first impression
Turnaround views are precise enough for 3D modeling reference
Expression sheet covers the emotional range needed for the character's game role
Color and material callouts use specific values, not vague descriptions
Scale reference establishes the character's size relative to the game world
Every major design choice is connected to the character's game role
Tradeoffs between readability, detail, and production cost are addressed
Target audience and genre conventions are considered and either followed or intentionally subverted
Writing is clear, concise, and professionally formatted
Cohesive character design with strong visual identity and readable silhouette
Rich personality with motivation depth and distinct character voice
Production-ready deliverables suitable for 3D pipeline handoff
Thoughtful design rationale connecting every choice to the character's game role

major_assignments.title: Animated Character with Companion AI Behavior Design
major_assignments.description: Take your character from Assignment 1 and bring them to life. Model and rig a simplified version of the character in Blender, create a set of 8 FACS-based facial blend shapes and a basic locomotion animation cycle, then design a complete companion AI behavior system including a behavior state machine diagram, 30 contextual barks organized by trigger condition, and a design document explaining how the character's personality informs their AI behavior.
objectives: Bridge the gap between character concept and in-engine character implementation
Apply FACS-based blend shapes to create expressive real-time facial animation
Design a companion AI behavior system grounded in the character's established personality
Demonstrate that visual design, animation style, and behavioral design all reinforce the same character identity
deliverables: Blender file with rigged character model, 8 FACS blend shapes, and locomotion cycle
30-second animation reel showing blend shapes in action and locomotion from multiple angles
Behavior state machine diagram with states, transitions, and trigger conditions
Contextual bark script: 30 lines organized by trigger (combat, exploration, idle, reaction, ambient)
Integration document (2-3 pages) explaining how animation and behavior choices reflect the character's personality

criteria: Character model matches the proportions and silhouette of the Assignment 1 character sheet
Rig deforms cleanly at major joints without mesh artifacts
Blend shapes produce natural facial expressions without clipping or distortion
Locomotion cycle has proper weight, timing, and foot contact
At least 8 FACS-based blend shapes covering core Action Units
Blend shapes combine additively to produce complex expressions
Expression transitions are smooth and avoid uncanny valley artifacts
Expressions match the emotional range defined in the Assignment 1 expression sheet
Behavior state machine is complete with clear states and transition conditions
30 contextual barks are varied, in-character, and organized by trigger type
Behavior design follows companion AI principles (no blocking, no kill-stealing, always present)
The character's personality is reflected in their behavioral priorities and reactions
Visual design, animation style, bark writing, and behavior priorities all tell the same story
Integration document identifies and resolves any inconsistencies between design layers
The character feels like one cohesive entity, not separate art and engineering artifacts
Portfolio presentation is professional and industry-ready
Character model and rig faithful to the original character sheet design
Expressive facial animation via FACS blend shapes with natural transitions
Comprehensive companion AI behavior design grounded in character personality
Seamless integration across visual, animation, and behavioral design layers

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes assessing understanding of character design theory, animation principles, and NPC design patterns

sketching_exercises.description: Weekly character sketching and design exercises building visual communication skills

major_assignments.description: Two major projects: a complete character design package and an animated character with companion AI design

peer_critiques.description: Structured critique sessions providing written feedback on classmates' character designs

final_portfolio.description: Professional portfolio compiling all character work with design rationale, suitable for industry presentation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all critique sessions and live demonstrations. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the participation grade by one full letter per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Design documents and written deliverables lose 10% per day late. Art deliverables (character sheets, animation reels) lose 15% per day late due to their role in peer critique scheduling. No submissions accepted more than 4 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: All character designs must be original individual work. You may use classmates as figure drawing references but may not base your character design on another student's work. Peer critique is collaborative; design execution is individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Reference material and artistic inspiration must be cited. Tracing over existing character art is prohibited. Use of AI image generation tools for final deliverables is not permitted; hand-drawn or hand-modeled work is required. AI tools may be used for brainstorming and reference gathering only, with disclosure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a digital drawing tablet (Wacom, Huion, or iPad with Apple Pencil), Blender 3.6+, and a drawing application (Photoshop, Clip Studio Paint, or Procreate). Lab drawing tablets are available for in-class use but personal hardware is strongly recommended for homework.
